God used Jonah again in his service. His using us shows that he is at peace with us. Jonah was not disobedient, as he had been. He neither tried to avoid hearing the command nor declined to obey it. Here is the nature of repentance: it is a change of mind and way, and a return to our work and duty. Also, the benefit of affliction: it brings back those who had deserted their place. See the power of Divine grace, for affliction itself would rather drive men from God than draw them to him. God's servants must go where he sends them, come when he calls them, and do what he bids them; we must do whatever the word of the Lord commands. Jonah faithfully and boldly delivered his message. Whether Jonah said more, to show the anger of God against them, or whether he only repeated these words again and again, is not certain, but this was the meaning of his message. Forty days is a long time for a righteous God to delay judgments, yet it is a short time for an unrighteous people to repent and reform. And should it not awaken us to get ready for death, considering that we cannot be so sure that we shall live forty days as Nineveh was that it would stand forty days? We should be alarmed if we were certain not to live a month, yet we are careless though we are not sure to live a day.

There was a wonder of Divine grace in the repentance and reformation of Nineveh. It condemns those of the gospel generation. A very small amount of light may convince people that humbling themselves before God, confessing their sins with prayer, and turning from sin are ways of escaping wrath and obtaining mercy. The people followed the example of the king. It became a national act, and it was necessary for it to be so when it was meant to prevent a national ruin. Let even the cries and moans of animals for lack of food remind their owners to pray to God. In prayer we must cry mightily, with fixedness of thought, firmness of faith, and devout affections. It concerns us in prayer to stir up all that is within us. It is not enough to fast for sin, but we must abstain from sin; and, so that our prayers may be successful, we must no longer regard iniquity in our hearts. The work of a fast day is not done when the day ends. The Ninevites hoped that God would turn away from his fierce anger; and that thus their ruin would be prevented. They could not be as confident of finding mercy upon their repentance as we may be, who have the death and merits of Christ, to which we may trust for pardon upon repentance. They dared not presume, but they did not despair. Hope of mercy is a great encouragement to repentance and reformation. Let us boldly cast ourselves down at the footstool of free grace, and God will look upon us with compassion. God sees who turn from their evil ways, and who do not. Thus he spared Nineveh. We read of no sacrifices offered to God to make atonement for sin; but a broken and contrite heart, such as the Ninevites then had, he will not despise.
